content:
# Leviticus Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 72v–73r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Command the Israelites to bring you clear oil pressed from olives for the light, so that the lamp can be kept burning continually. **3** Aaron is to set it up in the Tent of Meeting, outside the curtain that screens the Covenant chest, and tend it from evening until morning before the LORD. This is a permanent ordinance for all your generations. **4** He is to tend the lamps on the pure gold lampstand before the LORD continually.

**5** "Take the finest flour and bake twelve loaves of bread, using two-tenths of an ephah for each loaf. **6** Arrange them in two rows, six in each row, on the pure gold table before the LORD. **7** Place pure frankincense alongside each row. This is to serve as a token portion, a fire-offering to the LORD in place of the bread. **8** Every Sabbath, Aaron is to set it in order before the LORD — this is an everlasting covenant, a commitment on the part of the Israelites. **9** It belongs to Aaron and his sons; they are to eat it in a sacred place, for it is most holy — his portion of the fire-offerings of the LORD, given to him as a permanent right."

**10** Now a man whose mother was an Israelite and whose father was an Egyptian came out among the Israelites. In the camp, a fight broke out between this man of mixed descent and an Israelite. **11** During the brawl, the son of the Israelite woman blasphemed the Name and cursed it. So they brought him to Moses — his mother's name was Shelomith daughter of Dibri, of the tribe of Dan — **12** and they held him in custody while they waited to hear what the LORD would say.

**13** Then the LORD spoke to Moses: **14** "Bring the one who has done the cursing outside the camp. Let all who heard him lay their hands on his head, and then let the whole community stone him.

**15** "Tell the Israelites: Anyone who curses his God will bear responsibility for his sin. **16** Whoever blasphemes the name of the LORD must be put to death. The whole community is to stone him. Whether he is a foreigner or a native-born Israelite — when he blasphemes the Name, he must die.

**17** "Whoever takes a human life must be put to death. **18** Whoever kills another person's animal must make full restitution — a life for a life. **19** If anyone injures his neighbor, whatever he has done must be done to him in return: **20** fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth. The same injury he has inflicted on someone else must be inflicted on him. **21** Whoever kills an animal must make full restitution; whoever kills a person must be put to death.

**22** "You shall have one standard of justice — the same law applies to the foreigner as to the native-born. I am the LORD your God."

**23** Moses spoke to the Israelites, and they brought the one who had done the cursing outside the camp and stoned him. The Israelites did exactly what the LORD had commanded Moses.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### What Leviticus 24 Is Actually About

Leviticus 24 is one of the strangest chapters in the book — not because its content is bizarre, but because of how it is arranged. The chapter pivots from the purest, most exalted forms of worship (the perpetual light, the bread of the Presence) to the grittiest, most violent moment in Leviticus: a brawl in the camp, a blasphemer, and an execution. Then it swings back to legal principle — the lex talionis, equal justice for native and foreigner — before closing with the execution being carried out.

The juxtaposition is not accidental. The lamp burns before the LORD without ceasing. The bread of the Presence sits in his sight every day of every week. These rituals declare that Israel's life is lived continuously before the face of God. And it is against this backdrop of perpetual sacred presence that the blasphemy of the Name — the tearing of that veil — becomes intelligible as the act of ultimate rupture it is.

### Verses 1–4: The Perpetual Light — *Ner Tamid*

The instruction to bring "pure beaten olive oil" (*shemen zayit zakh katit*) for the menorah is not bureaucratic housekeeping. It is a statement about the character of worship. The oil must be *zakh* — clear, pure, free of sediment. The pressing must be *katit* — beaten, not ground or pressed in a mill; the finest, lightest oil comes from the first light beating of the olives, before the press crushes everything together. The care demanded in the oil's preparation mirrors the care demanded in every approach to the divine presence.

The lamp burns *tamid* — continually, perpetually, always. This word appears nine times in the instructions for the tabernacle's furnishings. The *ner tamid* (perpetual lamp) is still lit in synagogues today, suspended before the Torah ark, a direct descendant of this instruction. The fire that burned on the wilderness altar without ceasing (Lev 6:13) finds its counterpart in the lamp that burned in the holy place without ceasing. Darkness is never permitted to settle in the presence of the LORD.

Aaron tends the lamp "from evening until morning." This is the inverse of ordinary human schedules: while Israel sleeps, the priest is on duty before God. The holy place does not rest when the camp rests. Sacred tending is not constrained by human patterns of waking and sleeping.

### Verses 5–9: The Bread of the Presence — *Lechem ha-Panim*

The twelve loaves of showbread — literally "bread of the face" (*lechem ha-panim*), or "bread of the Presence" — are set out on a pure gold table in the holy place, two rows of six. They represent the twelve tribes: all of Israel, present before God in bread form. The loaves are renewed every Sabbath. The old loaves, removed on the Sabbath, are given to Aaron and his sons to eat in the holy place. They are most holy — so holy that even the eating of them is a sacred act, confined to sacred space.

The logic of the showbread is remarkable. God does not eat. The bread cannot be his food in any ordinary sense. Yet it sits before him every day of every week, as if he were a host at whose table Israel is a perpetual guest — or as if Israel were hosting God, setting a table in his honor week after week forever. The arrangement is mutual: Israel brings the bread; God provides the land whose grain makes the flour; the bread is consecrated by its presence before the face of God; the priests eat it as most holy food. The bread mediates between the ordinary life of Israel (who eat bread daily) and the holy presence of God (before whose face the bread is set).

The frankincense placed alongside each row serves as a *'azkarah* — a "memorial portion" or "token portion" — the part of the offering that is actually burned, going up as smoke to the LORD. The rest (the bread itself) goes to the priests. This same logic governs the grain offering of Leviticus 2: a portion is burned as memorial, and the remainder is sacred food.

The covenant language of verse 8 is striking: setting out the bread every Sabbath is called "an everlasting covenant" (*berit 'olam*). This is the vocabulary of the most solemn divine commitments — the covenant with Noah (Gen 9:16), with Abraham (Gen 17:7), with the priesthood of Phinehas (Num 25:13). The weekly bread is not a minor liturgical detail; it is a covenant obligation, a relationship-defining act that is renewed every seven days as long as Israel endures.

### Verses 10–13: The Narrative Interruption — A Fight, a Blasphemer, a Question

The sudden shift in verse 10 is one of the most startling narrative pivots in the Pentateuch. The text has been operating at the level of timeless instruction — "command the Israelites," "this shall be a permanent ordinance" — and then, without warning, it drops into a specific story: a particular man, in a particular fight, on a particular day in the wilderness camp.

The man's identity is carefully specified: his mother was an Israelite woman (Shelomith daughter of Dibri of the tribe of Dan); his father was Egyptian. He is a son of mixed heritage — the kind of person the laws of Leviticus consistently include in the covenant community (the foreigner who sojourns among you, the mixed multitude who came out of Egypt with Israel). His dual identity is not presented as a cause or an excuse for what he does; it is presented as context, and the legal ruling that follows will address his case precisely because it is a borderline case: what law applies to someone like him?

What happens in the fight? The Hebrew says *va-yiqqov... et-ha-Shem* — literally "he pierced/designated the Name" — and then *va-yeqallel*, "he cursed." The verb *naqav* (to pierce, to designate by name, to blaspheme) is unusual in this context. It may mean he pronounced the divine Name aloud — not merely used a curse formula invoking God, but actually spoke the unutterable Name itself — in a context of contempt or rage. The gravity of the act is not entirely clear to those who witnessed it; that is why they bring him to Moses and wait for clarification. The act sits in a legal gray zone, which is exactly why it triggers this narrative.

Moses and the community hold the man in custody while waiting for divine instruction. This is the same procedure as the case of the sabbath-breaker (Num 15:34) and the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27:5): when a case exceeds existing law, Moses brings it before the LORD and waits. The legal system of Israel is not closed; it is responsive to new situations.

### Verses 14–16: The Verdict — Death for Blasphemy

The sentence is communal: the whole congregation participates in the execution. Before the stoning, all those who heard the blasphemy lay their hands on the blasphemer's head. This gesture, which elsewhere transfers sin from person to sacrificial animal (Lev 4:4; 16:21), here transfers the weight of the witnessed offense back onto its source. The witnesses are cleared; the guilt rests fully and finally on the one who committed the act.

The law is then universalized in verse 16: it applies equally to the foreigner (*ger*) and the native-born (*ezrach*). This is the legal conclusion drawn from the specific case: the man of mixed heritage is not in a legal no-man's-land. The same law that would govern an Israelite governs him. The covenant community's most solemn obligation — reverence for the Name — is binding on everyone who lives within it, regardless of ancestry.

The severity of the punishment for blasphemy must be read within the theological logic of the Holiness Code. The entire system of Leviticus rests on the reality of the divine Name and the divine Presence. The tabernacle is not a symbol of God; God actually dwells there. The rituals of atonement and holiness are not merely cultural practices; they maintain the relational fabric between a holy God and an increasingly holy people. To blaspheme the Name is to tear that fabric at its center — to attack the ground of the whole project of redemption and covenant. It is not punished as a thought-crime or a breach of etiquette; it is punished as the most fundamental act of communal destruction.

### Verses 17–22: The Lex Talionis — Eye for Eye

The lex talionis — the law of retaliation — is one of the most widely quoted and most commonly misunderstood passages in the Bible. "Eye for eye, tooth for tooth" (v.20) has become a byword for primitive revenge. But its original function in the ancient legal context is almost exactly the opposite of revenge: it is a law of proportionality designed to *limit* retaliation.

In the ancient Near East, injury to a powerful person could lead to wildly disproportionate responses. The law of exact equivalence — only a fracture for a fracture, only an eye for an eye — is a cap on retaliation, not a license for it. You may not take a life for an eye. You may not take more than is equivalent. The lex talionis is a ceiling, not a floor.

The Hebrew root here is *ta'chat* — "in place of," "instead of." An eye *in place of* an eye. The structure is one of replacement, not accumulation. You lose what you caused another to lose; you do not also lose more.

In the ancient Near Eastern context (and, it is likely, in the actual practice of Israelite courts), the lex talionis was often applied through monetary compensation: the value of an eye, the value of a tooth, assessed and paid. The Talmud develops this extensively. The principle is not that courts should literally gouge out eyes; it is that the compensation must be exactly equivalent to the loss. No more, no less.

The sequence of verses 17–21 moves from the most extreme case (taking a human life, v.17) to injury to humans (vv.19–20) to killing animals (vv.18, 21), then returns to the capital case (taking a human life, v.21). The repetition is structural and deliberate: it frames the talionic principle between two statements about the most serious offense. Human life is not just at the top of the scale; it bookends and anchors the entire legal section.

### Verse 22: One Law for All — *Mishpat Echad*

"You shall have one standard of justice — the same law applies to the foreigner as to the native-born." This single verse is one of the most radical legal principles in the ancient world. Virtually every other ancient legal code (Hammurabi's, the Hittite laws, the Assyrian laws) operates on a two-tier system: higher-status persons receive greater protection, and punishments vary based on the social rank of victim and perpetrator.

Leviticus 24:22 demolishes this tiered logic. The foreigner (*ger*) is not a second-class legal person. The same law that protects and binds the native Israelite protects and binds the foreigner. The blasphemy law of verses 15–16 is the first application; but the principle is stated broadly enough to cover the entire preceding legal section, and by implication the entire Holiness Code.

The theological grounding is, as always, the divine identity: "I am the LORD your God." The equality of all persons before the law is rooted not in a philosophical theory of human dignity but in the character of the God who gave the law. He is LORD of both native and foreigner. His standards are not relative to social position. His justice does not have a sliding scale.

### The Architecture of the Chapter: Light, Bread, Fire

Read in its final canonical form, Leviticus 24 has a subtle theological architecture that connects all three of its sections.

The menorah burns before the LORD *tamid* — perpetually. The bread sits before his *panim* — his face — every day of every week. The LORD's presence is continuous, attentive, and close. His face does not turn away. His fire does not go out.

Against this backdrop of perpetual presence, the blasphemy is not merely an act of impiety. It is an act of willful rupture — a declaration that the Name does not matter, that the Presence is not real, or that the covenant relationship can be torn without consequence. The fire that burns continually in the sanctuary and the fire that falls on the blasphemer are, in the chapter's logic, the same fire: the holy fire of a God who is always, without ceasing, present.

The lex talionis and the law of equal justice (vv.17–22) extend this logic into human community. Just as God's justice does not vary based on who you are, so Israel's courts must not vary their standard based on ancestry or status. The perpetual lamp and the equal law are expressions of the same theological reality: a God whose presence is consistent, whose character is unchanging, and whose justice is not for sale.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 110–111)

Vaticanus pages 110–111 preserve the Greek of Leviticus 24. The chapter opens in the left column of page 110, running into the middle column, with the narrative section continuing across to page 111. The manuscript is well-preserved across these folios.

The LXX translates *lechem ha-panim* as *artous tēs protheseōs* — "the loaves of the setting forth" or "the loaves of presentation." The Greek word *prothesis* (setting forth, presentation, placing before) is the same word Paul uses in Romans 8:28 for God's "purpose" (*prothesis*) and in Hebrews 9:2 for the table of showbread itself. When the author of Hebrews catalogs the tabernacle furnishings, he uses the LXX vocabulary here — the bread table is the *trapeza tēs protheseōs*, the table of presentation. The bread of Leviticus 24 is directly in view in the New Testament's theology of the tabernacle as a shadow of heavenly realities.

The Greek rendering of *va-yiqqov et-ha-Shem* (he blasphemed the Name) is *onomazō to onoma* — literally "he named the Name." This rendering captures the paradox of blasphemy: the divine Name is so holy that even to name it in the wrong context is to violate it. The Name is not just a label; it is a presence. To speak it carelessly or contemptibly is to assault the Presence itself.

The lex talionis passage in the LXX (v.20) is rendered with the same exact-equivalence vocabulary: *syntagma anti syntasmatos, ophthalmos anti ophthalmou, odous anti odontos* — "fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth." The Greek *anti* (instead of, in place of) carries the same substitution logic as the Hebrew *tachat*: not accumulation, but exact replacement. The LXX makes the proportionality principle absolutely explicit.

The closing verse of the chapter in the LXX ends with the note that the Israelites did *kathaper synetaxen kyrios tō Mōysē* — "just as the LORD commanded Moses." The obedience is total and immediate. The chapter opens with a commandment about perpetual light and perpetual bread; it closes with a community acting in perpetual obedience. The *tamid* — the perpetual quality of Israel's life before God — runs through the whole.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 72v–73r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 110–111 (LXX).*
